html:
GENFIT is no longer a failed NASH biotech. It is a complicated, under-followed residual royalty vehicle built around Iqirvo, Ipsen’s fast-ramping PBC drug, with a cash-funded liver-disease pipeline attached.

The market’s stale label still matters. GENFIT voluntarily delisted its ADSs from Nasdaq in late 2025, the old U.S. retail holder base largely disappeared, and the F:XUPB ADR line is now a poor valuation anchor. Each ADS represented one ordinary share, so the cleaner economic reference is Euronext Paris GNFT, last quoted at €8.60 on May 15, 2026, implying roughly €430 million of equity value.

The commercial data have changed the story. Iqirvo generated $208 million of 2025 net sales, including $88 million in Q4, triggering GENFIT’s first $20 million commercial milestone ahead of schedule. Ipsen’s Q1 2026 update showed Rare Disease sales up 125% at constant currency, with French market discussion and analyst parsing pointing to about €79 million, or roughly $91 million, of Q1 Iqirvo sales. That annualizes near $360 million before further European launch maturity. This is not an early science-project royalty. It is an approved, marketed drug with a visible launch curve.

The market structure improved in late 2025 when Ocaliva was withdrawn from the U.S. PBC market after FDA pressure. The remaining branded second-line PBC field is essentially Iqirvo versus Gilead’s Livdelzi, plus off-label fibrates and reimbursement friction. Livdelzi is a serious competitor, especially on pruritus, but Iqirvo does not need monopoly economics. A two-player chronic orphan market can still support $600 million to $900 million peak sales if diagnosis, second-line treatment rates, and European access keep improving.

The main reason the stock is still cheap is that the economics are messy. GENFIT signed a capped royalty financing with HCRx. HCRx receives a portion of Iqirvo royalties until return caps are met, while GENFIT retains milestones and later royalty reversion. That means GENFIT is not a clean near-term free-cash-flow royalty company. But the complexity is also the opportunity: if Iqirvo disappoints, HCRx captures much of the early stream; if Iqirvo wins, the financing is repaid faster, later royalties revert to GENFIT, sales above the annual cap become more valuable, and retained commercial milestones arrive without equity dilution.

The balance sheet is stronger than the IFRS optics. GENFIT ended 2025 with €101 million of cash, then received a €30 million royalty-financing tranche in January 2026, with the $20 million Iqirvo milestone not yet in year-end cash. The company says cash runway extends beyond 2028. The reported 2025 loss was distorted by a non-cash VS-01 ACLF impairment and fair-value accounting tied to a better royalty forecast. A screen sees negative equity and biotech losses. The real question is whether a roughly €430 million market cap is too low for pro forma liquidity, retained milestones, post-HCRx royalty reversion, and several pipeline shots.

The pipeline should not drive the base case, but it is not worthless. NTZ/G1090N received FDA orphan drug designation for ACLF in March 2026 and is targeted for Phase 2 initiation in H2 2026. GNS561 in cholangiocarcinoma has mid-2026 data and a possible Phase 2 start in H2. The MASH diagnostic franchise got a reimbursement step as the MASH treatment market expands. The biggest free option is PSC: Ipsen has initiated a global Phase 3 program for elafibranor in a disease with no approved therapies, and GENFIT would be eligible for additional milestones and double-digit royalties if that indication works.

Retail context is oddly bullish but still narrow. French boards are actively modeling Iqirvo quarterly sales and royalty value, while U.S. social attention is thin after the Nasdaq exit. Patient chatter shows real access friction, including high copays even after approval, but that concern is already visible in the launch and has not stopped strong early uptake.

The bear case is not zero, but it is real. If Livdelzi takes most new starts, payers force restrictive access, ELSPIRE disappoints in H2 2026, or GENFIT burns the cash pile into weak R&D, the royalty reversion thesis gets pushed out. The HCRx structure makes near-term cash conversion worse than a simple royalty-company screen would imply. The ADS delisting also makes liquidity and ownership quality worse.

Still, the forward setup is asymmetric. At the current €8.60 share price, the market is paying about €430 million for a company with roughly €145 million of pro forma liquidity before 2026 burn, a drug already annualizing near $360 million in sales, retained milestones, and a capped royalty financing that becomes less punitive as Iqirvo sells more.

Bear target: €6.50, about 24% downside. Iqirvo stalls around $300 million to $350 million of annual sales, HCRx absorbs most royalty economics for years, no meaningful pipeline value emerges, and cash burn offsets milestones.

Base target: €18, about 109% upside. Iqirvo reaches $600 million to $700 million peak PBC sales, GENFIT collects further commercial milestones, HCRx is worked through on a reasonable timeline, and the market values the residual royalty plus cash-funded pipeline at roughly €900 million.

Bull target: €32, about 272% upside. Iqirvo becomes a leading second-line PBC therapy, ELSPIRE removes confirmatory-trial overhang, PSC becomes credible, sales above the HCRx annual cap matter, and the post-financing royalty stream is valued like a scarce orphan-drug annuity rather than a failed biotech remnant.
